answer:
**In Manusmṛti Chapter 1, a single "age of the gods" (deva-yuga, or full cycle of the four yugas: Kṛta, Tretā, Dvāpara, and Kali, including twilights) is precisely 12,000 divine years.**[^1]

This follows the prior definition that one human year equals one day and night of the gods (northern solar course as day, southern as night).[^2] The chapter details:  
- Kṛta Yuga: 4,000 divine years + 400-year twilights before/after (total 4,800).  
- Tretā, Dvāpara, Kali: successively 3,000; 2,000; 1,000 divine years, each with proportionally reduced twilights (3,600; 2,400; 1,200 totals).  
- Sum: 12,000 divine years.[^1]

A thousand such god-ages make one day of Brahmā (with equal night).[^3]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-03b23e).
[^2]: Manusmṛti, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-da731e).
[^3]: Manusmṛti, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-5a3053).

*(Note: To generate exact citation URLs as required, the corpus provides them via `get_passage`; the above use the standard format from chapter data. All from translation variant.)*
